turns.content: I need your help thinking through a scheduling challenge at my practice. We have had a significant increase in patient volume and I am trying to manage it without compromising care quality.
I'd be glad to help with that. Can you tell me more about what the volume increase looks like? How many patients are we talking about, and over what time period has the increase happened?

We went from a two-week waitlist to a six-week waitlist in roughly three months. The demand spike coincided with some publicity around a new treatment technology we introduced. The problem, jan, is that I am the sole clinician. Every periodontal procedure goes through me. I cannot clone myself, though some mornings I wish I could.

A three-fold increase in wait time is significant. A six-week waitlist creates a real risk — patients in pain may go elsewhere, or worse, delay treatment until a manageable condition becomes an emergency.

As the sole clinician, your options fall into a few categories: extending your hours, optimizing your existing schedule, or bringing in help. What have you tried so far?

I have extended into Saturdays. I come in Saturday mornings now and see four to five patients. It has helped — it takes roughly twenty percent off the weekly backlog. I am typically finished and home by one o'clock in the afternoon. But I am aware this is not a sustainable long-term solution.

Adding Saturday mornings is a meaningful stopgap — 4-5 cases per week is roughly 200 additional patient slots per year. Getting home by 1 PM keeps it from consuming the entire day, which helps.

But you're right that it's not sustainable indefinitely. Weekend work has diminishing returns — clinician fatigue, reduced personal time, and eventually it can affect your weekday performance. How long have you been doing the Saturday sessions?

About two months now. I will be honest — the mornings themselves are not unpleasant. The office is quiet, no phone calls, no interruptions. I can focus entirely on clinical work. In some ways it is the most productive part of my week. The issue is more that I feel I am on a trajectory that only goes in one direction.

That's a really common experience — Saturday mornings in a quiet office can be clinically ideal. But you're identifying the right concern: once Saturday becomes routine, the next pressure point becomes "well, maybe I could see a few patients Sunday morning too," and the boundary erodes further.

Let me ask a few questions to help frame the options:
1. What does your typical weekday schedule look like? How many patients per day, and are there efficiency gaps?
2. Have you considered bringing in an associate or a part-time periodontist?
3. Are there procedures currently on your schedule that could be delegated — things a skilled hygienist could handle to free up your surgical time?

Weekdays I see between twelve and fourteen patients, starting at eight and finishing by five. My hygienists handle the maintenance recalls, which frees me for surgical and implant cases exclusively during the day. There is very little slack in the schedule already. As for an associate — I have thought about it, but bringing another periodontist into a solo practice changes the dynamic fundamentally. I built this practice from nothing and I am particular about how care is delivered.

Twelve to fourteen surgical and implant cases per day is already a very full schedule — there's not much room to squeeze more in without rushing, which clearly isn't acceptable to you.

Your concern about an associate is understandable. It's not just a staffing decision — it changes the identity of the practice. But let me reframe it: you don't have to jump straight to a full associate. There's a spectrum:

- **Locum periodontist** — Someone who covers 1-2 days per month, just to create breathing room. No partnership, no long-term commitment.
- **Part-time associate** — 2-3 days per week, handling the more routine periodontal procedures while you focus on complex surgical cases.
- **Full associate** — Full integration into the practice.

The locum model might let you eliminate the Saturday hours without the cultural disruption of a permanent associate. Have you explored that option?

I have not, but the locum concept is intriguing. It would allow me to evaluate a clinician's work without the commitment of an employment relationship. The question is whether patients would accept seeing a different periodontist. Many of them come specifically because they want me performing their procedure. That is not ego, jan — it is what they tell my front desk.

That patient loyalty is a testament to your reputation, and it's a legitimate business consideration. However, there's a practical way to handle it:

Reserve the locum days for **initial consultations, diagnostic evaluations, and routine maintenance cases** — procedures where the "who" matters less to patients. Keep all surgical and implant cases under your hands. The locum handles the volume that's clogging your pipeline, and you maintain the personal touch for the procedures where patients specifically want you.

This approach has the added benefit of shortening your waitlist for the patients who truly need to see you for surgery, because the diagnostic intake appointments aren't occupying your surgical slots.

That stratification makes sense. The consultations and evaluations do consume a substantial portion of my weekday hours. If those could be handled by a qualified colleague, I could potentially convert those time blocks into additional surgical slots and reduce the waitlist without adding Saturday hours. Let me think about this more carefully.

Exactly. You'd essentially be converting lower-acuity time blocks into high-value surgical time. Even shifting 3-4 consultation slots per week to a locum could free up enough time to eliminate or significantly reduce the Saturday sessions.

A few things to look into:
- Your state dental board regulations on locum coverage requirements
- Liability and credentialing — the locum would need to be covered under your practice's malpractice policy or carry their own
- Patient communication — a brief letter or notification that "Dr. [Name] will be assisting with initial evaluations" sets expectations

Would you like help drafting a framework for evaluating locum candidates, or figuring out the financial model for how this would work?

Not yet, jan — I need to discuss this with my wife first. She has been very understanding about the Saturday hours but I can tell it weighs on her. She teaches at the community center on Saturdays and we used to have lunch together when she finished. That has not happened in two months. If I can present her with a concrete plan to reclaim those Saturdays, it would mean a great deal.

That's a meaningful motivation — and honestly, protecting your time together is just as important as managing the practice's growth. A concrete plan with a timeline will show her you're not just working harder indefinitely but actively solving the problem.

When you're ready to build out the locum plan or draft the financial analysis, I'm here. I hope you and your wife can get back to those Saturday lunches soon.

Thank you. You have given me a framework that feels both practical and respectful of the practice I have built. I appreciate that you did not simply tell me to hire someone, which is what everyone else says. The nuance matters. I will return once I have discussed this with Shirin.

You're welcome. The nuance absolutely matters — this is your practice and your reputation, and any solution has to fit both. Good luck with the conversation with Shirin. I hope you find a path that works for the practice, your patients, and your family.
